levels.label: Experimenting
levels.copy: The AI Adoption Readiness pattern is still mostly informal or ad hoc. Pick one stabilising action, document it, and avoid adding a long process before the basics are real.

levels.label: Opportunistic
levels.copy: Some AI Adoption Readiness practices exist, but they still depend on individual memory and effort. The next step is two repeatable routines with clear owners.

levels.label: Piloting
levels.copy: Core AI Adoption Readiness practices are repeatable, but evidence quality and ownership are uneven. Work on the weakest dimension before optimising the strongest one.

levels.label: Scaling
levels.copy: AI Adoption Readiness practices are measured and connected to decisions. The main work is now removing the constraint that prevents the system from scaling cleanly.

levels.label: AI-Native Operator
levels.copy: AI Adoption Readiness is operating as a learning system. Keep calibrating against evidence, stress-test resilience, and avoid treating a strong score as a permanent guarantee.

recommendations.use-cases-and-value: Prioritise three use cases with baseline, owner, value hypothesis, risk, and kill criteria.
recommendations.data-and-technology: Create an approved data/model architecture with lineage, access, evaluation, and fallback requirements.
recommendations.people-and-workflow: Redesign one workflow end to end and certify role-specific human review responsibilities.
recommendations.governance-and-measurement: Establish an AI inventory, risk tiers, approval owner, and post-deployment measurement dashboard.

defaultSecondAction: Retake the diagnostic after a focused 30-day improvement cycle and compare only your own progress unless a team comparison is explicitly agreed.
reflectionQuestion: Which assumption behind your weakest dimension deserves the fastest evidence check?
methodologyNote: The current version uses four dimensions with three questions each. Dimension scores are normalised to 0-100. The balanced index weights 70% average dimension performance and 30% the weakest dimension. No percentile or benchmark is shown until a compatible sample exists.
shareNote: Share cards reveal only the level and strongest area. They do not publish individual answers, sensitive weaknesses, legal or financial details, security gaps, or family-specific information.
